_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Lawrence County, OH. County formed 1815 from Gallia and Scioto counties. Heart of the Hanging Rock Iron Region. No major courthouse fire — records run from formation.

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat was at Burlington on the Ohio River. Records transferred to Ironton when the seat moved in 1851.
First Ironton courthouse. Replaced by the 1908 building. No catastrophic fire damage.

record_losses.cause: No major fire or flood damage to county records
record_losses.description: Lawrence County's courthouse has not experienced a major fire or flood that destroyed records. Records run continuously from the 1815 formation. The Hanging Rock Iron Region generated supplementary records — iron furnace account books, company records, and employment documentation — that exist outside the courthouse. Some minor deterioration of early volumes but no catastrophic loss.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Lawrence County's records survive from 1815. If {ancestor_name} was in the county, the deed books, will books, and court minutes are available. Gaps in the record are more likely to reflect the ancestor's circumstances — iron furnace workers and coal miners moved frequently and may not appear in property records.
record_losses.source: Ohio Historical Society. Guide to County Records in the Ohio Historical Society Archives.

deeds.repository: Lawrence County Recorder's Office; Ohio History Connection microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose estate papers are fragmentary.
wills_estates.repository: Lawrence County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection

marriages.repository: Lawrence County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection microfilm

court_minutes.repository: Lawrence County Clerk of Courts; Ohio History Connection

vital_records.completeness: Uneven until early 1900s. Iron furnace communities and coal camps may be underreported.
vital_records.repository: Lawrence County Health Department; Ohio Department of Health

substitute_sources.description: Iron furnace operations maintained account books, employment records, and production logs. Where these survive, they document workers, wages, and the furnace community.
substitute_sources.repository: Ohio History Connection; Briggs Lawrence County Public Library local history collection; some records in private family collections of ironmaster families
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for identifying iron workers — founders, colliers, miners, and teamsters. Furnace records may document workers who do not appear in county property records because they lived in company housing.
substitute_sources.source: Stout, Wilber, and R. A. Schoenlaub. The Iron Ore Resources of Ohio. Columbus: Ohio Geological Survey, 1945.

substitute_sources.description: Lawrence County enumerated from 1820. Manufacturing schedules document iron furnace operations.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential. The manufacturing schedules for 1850–1880 provide detailed information about iron furnace operations — number of employees, capital invested, and output. Slave schedules are not applicable (Ohio was free soil) but the census documents free Black workers at the furnaces.

substitute_sources.source_type: Church records — Methodist, Baptist, and Welsh chapel records
substitute_sources.description: Methodist and Baptist churches were the primary denominations. Some Welsh chapel records exist from the Welsh settlement communities.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Supplements civil records for vital events.

narrative_hooks: Lawrence County's records run from 1815 with no major losses. If {ancestor_name} owned property or appeared in court, the record is there. The harder question is the iron workers — men who lived in company housing, bought at the company store, and might not appear in the county records at all.

The Hanging Rock iron furnace account books — where they survive — document workers the county recorder never saw. If {ancestor_name} was a collier, a founder, or a teamster at a Lawrence County furnace, the furnace records may be the only documentation of {pronoun_possessive} working life.

Lawrence County's iron furnaces employed free Black workers alongside white workers — in a county on the Ohio River border with slave-state Kentucky. If {ancestor_name} was Black in Lawrence County, the furnace records and the census are the primary sources.

Iron furnace workers moved with the industry — when a furnace went cold, the workers moved to the next one. {ancestor_name} may appear in Lawrence County records for a few years and then vanish, not because {pronoun_subject} died but because {pronoun_subject} followed the work.
source_references: Ohio Historical Society. Guide to County Records in the Ohio Historical Society Archives.
Stout, Wilber, and R. A. Schoenlaub. The Iron Ore Resources of Ohio. Columbus: Ohio Geological Survey, 1945.
Briggs Lawrence County Public Library. Local History Collection finding aids.
